issue_title: Inline table support
issue_body:
When I try to parse a file that contains an inline table (as defined by [toml 0.4](https://github.com/toml-lang/toml#user-content-inline-table), the parser crashes. For intance, with:
```
origins_to_ranks = {nomderue = 20, communes = 10}
```

I get.
```
  File "/home/jenselme/Stage3A/geo-api3/chsdi/tests/functional/test_sphinx.py", line 26, in test_sphinx_api_query
    config = toml.load(config_file)
  File "/home/jenselme/Stage3A/geo-api3/.venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/toml.py", line 43, in load
    return loads(f.read(), _dict)
  File "/home/jenselme/Stage3A/geo-api3/.venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/toml.py", line 331, in loads
    value, vtype = load_value(pair[1])
  File "/home/jenselme/Stage3A/geo-api3/.venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/toml.py", line 453, in load_value
    parsed_date = load_date(v)
  File "/home/jenselme/Stage3A/geo-api3/.venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/toml.py", line 350, in load_date
    tz = TomlTz(val[19:24])
  File "/home/jenselme/Stage3A/geo-api3/.venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/toml.py", line 9, in __new__
    self._hours = int(toml_offset[:3])

```

Can you add support for this syntax please?

issue_title: Consider a backport to Python 2.7?
issue_body:
Here are some initial thoughts but definitely incomplete list of changes necessary.

* Switch hard coded `bytes` to be configurable to `str`.
* Loss of `int.to_bytes()` and `int.from_bytes()`.
* Loss of `datetime.timestamp()`
* Differences between `memoryview` and `buffer`?

issue_title: Add support for __int__
issue_body: In Python, a conversion to an integer should use `__int__` if no conversion is known. If the `mpz` function does not know how to convert something to an `mpz`, it should try `PyNumber_Int`.

issue_title: Why are non-.py files ignored?
issue_body:
The Unix philosphy is to hide interpreter choice inside an executable file (ie magic or #!).  It needn't have a file extension.  If you do this, you can swap implementations say, between sh and py and C and not change the user's view.

So if you have a directory containing a perfectly cromulent py program,

```
#!/usr/bin/python
import requests
print requests.get("http://ifconfig.co/ip").text
```

pipreqs will not find requests.  Is this intentional?  Why not have a "--examine-all" switch or make such the default?

issue_title: alias produces invalid code for fish as of 3.24
Instructions on complete uninstallation?
issue_body:
Hello,

I do not use any of the supported package managers, so I used the wget command to install fuck. (I didn't realize that it was going to install pip as part of that command, but I guess I should have been paying more attention.)

Anyway, I decided that it's not for me, and removed the line about configuring it from my .bashrc and ran pip uninstall thefuck (or something similar) to uninstall it, which seemed to run successfully. I also removed the "thefuck" directory from my .config folder.

Unfortunately, now every time I open a terminal or access the console I immediately get the message "-bash: thefuck: command not found", so there is still something looking for thefuck every time I use a console. Any idea how to remove this behavior? There doesn't seem to be much included in the readme about uninstallation

issue_title: Default for escape in Math is None but default for dumps_list is True
issue_body:
In math.py:
```python
class Math(Container):
(......)

    def __init__(self, *, inline=False, data=None, escape=None):
        r"""
        Args
        ----
        (......)
        escape : bool
            if True, will escape strings
        """
        (......)

    def dumps(self):
        (......)
        if self.inline:
            return '$' + self.dumps_content() + '$'
        return '\\[%\n' + self.dumps_content() + '%\n\\]'
``` 

but in container class:
```python
from pylatex.utils import dumps_list
(......)
    def dumps_content(self, **kwargs):
        (......)
        return dumps_list(self, escape=self.escape,
                          token=self.content_separator, **kwargs)
```
and then in pylatex.utils:
```python
def dumps_list(l, *, escape=True, token='%\n', mapper=None, as_content=True):
    r"""Try to generate a LaTeX string of a list that can contain anything.
```

So, finally, the math expression is being escaped by default. Please make this behaviour clear in the Math class itself. Thanks!

issue_title: bulkCmd() returning varbind not in scope of requested OID when maxRepetitions == (numSubElements + 1)
issue_body:
we recently switched from easysnmp to pysnmp just because of the excellent MIB services. I'm quite happy with pysnmp, although we needed to fix at least 3 to 4 issues in various Cisco MIBs. But anyway, using the hlapi in conjunction with the MIB sevices is a great pleasure!

Having said that we came across an issue in a very particular situation:
Calling cmdgen.CommandGenerator().bulkCmd(...) with maxRepititions set to a number that is exactly one above the number of elements to be returned from the last SNMP GETBULK response will return exaxtly one varbind to much. The surplus varbind contains the next OID not in the scope of the requested OID with an empty value. So, if you only check for the last element in the returned OID (the index) and not the whole OID you will most likely get an index of 1 and thus clear your first result when copying out the results.

Example: if you do a bulkCmd()  for "IF-MIB::ifDescr" with maxRepititions set to 10 (or 5) and it happens that you should get 9 varbinds (interface descriptions) you will get 10 varbinds, the last being not in the scope of "IF-MIB::ifDescr". 

Also, I observed in Wireshark that in this particular situation bulkCmd() will do one SNMP GETBULK too much.

I'm _attaching a python script to check this issue:

[getIfDescrs.py.txt](https://github.com/etingof/pysnmp/files/2200920/getIfDescrs.py.txt)

issue_title: position error
issue_body:
Hi Philippe,

Thank you so much for sharing your excellent work. I quite like this library and did some experiments on that. I find some weird issues concerning position property.

Here is the code:

import bt
data = bt.get('spy, ashr, agg, eem, ewz', start='2014-01-01', end='2014-02-02')

class PrintAlgo(bt.Algo):
    def __init__(self):
        super(PrintAlgo, self).__init__()
    
    def __call__(self, target):
        print '\n' + '*' * 20 + str(target.now) + '*' * 20
        print target.positions
        print pd.DataFrame({x.name: x.positions for x in target.members
                                 if isinstance(x, bt.core.SecurityBase)})
        return True

s = bt.Strategy('s1', [
                       bt.algos.RunWeekly(),
                       bt.algos.SelectAll(),
                       bt.algos.WeighEqually(),
                       bt.algos.Rebalance(),
                       bt.algos.run_always(PrintAlgo()),
                        ])

test = bt.Backtest(s, data, initial_capital=80000)
res = bt.run(test)

issue_title: Hard coded remote names
issue_body:
I've only started playing, and it seems like the remote 'gitlab' is special, and everything else is ignored.  I suspect the same occurs for github and others.

This means `git-repo` cant be immediately used in a git clone of a gitlab repo that was cloned using `git clone`, as that will use the remote name `origin`.

It would be nice if `git-repo` handled a missing `gitlab` remote by looking for another suitable remote that points to gitlab, with a sensible strategy that works in most cases.

The most obvious fallback is to look to see if `origin` is a gitlab repo, and use that.

Then looking at all remotes, and see if there is only one that points to gitlab, then that should be the default used.

`git-repo` could then create a `gitlab` remote if it needs to avoid expensive calls again, so the `gitlab` remote becomes more of a cached default remote, rather than the only one that works.

issue_title: Upload-like requests where body.read() doesn't return the full file (i.e. it returns small buffers) hang VCRpy
issue_body:
If the request "body" is a file-like object where body.read() doesn't return the full file (i.e. I am testing a proxy where "body" is actually a socket that reads from the client and body.read() return fixed length buffers), the request hangs when running through VCRpy.

The problem is here (vcrpy/request.py: __init__):

        self._was_file = hasattr(body, 'read')
        if self._was_file:
            self.body = body.read()

where you do a single read. The request to the real server probably hangs because the Content-Length specify the full size of the file, but the posted body is shorter (so the server is waiting for more bytes, while the client is waiting for the response).

The correct solution should be to keep self.body as something the request can "read from", but a working solution, easier to implement, could be to at least do a "loop" of reads:

  while True:
    ret = body.read()
    if ret == '':
       break
    self.body += ret

issue_title: Feature request: Generators in processes
issue_body:
## User story
As a developer I would like to run a generator in another process, and get the results one by one to be able to process them in the main thread.

The function I'm running generates a results which uses a lot of RAM (a list with many objects) and each object of the list can be processed individually. If I wouldn't be using a different process to run the function that generates these results, I would certainly use a generator function.

At the moment the functions being run in the sub-processes can only return one result: the list with all the objects. In an ideal scenario, if this gets implemented, `futures.result()` would return a generator which I could iterate over. Each time a result is produced by the sub-process function, it is pickled, sent to the pipe, unpickled and yield in the generator.

## Notes
 * I know I could do this manually somehow: use files to store the objects, use memcached, use an external queue system, etc.
 * If it is already implemented, please let me know where :+1: 
 * If there is an easier way to do this, please let me know how :+1:

issue_title: Jedi not correctly caching since v0.10.0?
issue_body:
I've been digging through jedi's source to try and find out why completions in `jedi-vim` appeared to have slowed down a lot. I think this is related to https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/issues/915, but I just wanted to add this in case it's useful. I used the following small example of getting completions for `matplotlib`

`comp.py`:
```
# coding: utf-8
import time
import jedi

source = """
import matplotlib
matplotlib."""
script = jedi.Script(source, 3, len("matplotlib."))

start = time.time()
script.completions()
end = time.time()
print(end - start)

start = time.time()
script.completions()
end = time.time()
print(end - start)
```

And checked the execution time with different versions of `jedi`:

```

issue_title: Nested ".config" directory inside ".config/wal/colorschemes"
issue_body:
Is this intentional? When I create `.config/wal/colorschemes` to put custom colorschemes in there, `pywal` seems to create an extra `.config/wal/colorschemes` inside the initial `colorschemes` folder. So now there's another `.config` directory in the same location with my custom colorschemes, inside which is another hierarchy: `.config/wal/colorschemes/`, inside `.config/wal/colorschemes`

Another thing I've just spotted that might be related to this: if there's no `colorschemes` folder inside `.config/wal`, running `wal --theme` will output errors:

`Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/bin/wal", line 11, in <module>
    load_entry_point('pywal==2.0.5', 'console_scripts', 'wal')()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pywal/__main__.py", line 187, in main
    parse_args_exit(parser)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pywal/__main__.py", line 120, in parse_args_exit
    for theme in theme.list_themes()]
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pywal/theme.py", line 15, in list_themes
    themes = [*os.scandir(os.path.join(CONF_DIR, "colorschemes")),
FileNotFoundError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/home/xxxxxx/.config/wal/colorschemes'`

`pywal` could have just created the `colorschemes` folder if it didn't exist, right?

To sumup, it seems like: if the `colorschemes` folder doesn't exist inside `.config/wal`, `pywal` will throw errors without doing anything; and if the `colorschemes` folder is there, `pywal` will create another folder of the same hierarchy: `.config/wal/colorschemes` inside it.

issue_title: Passing args kwargs from request into is_authenticated method cause unexpected behaviour
issue_body:
There are two cases, one of them is the real problem, another is associated.

The first problem appears when you try to use `protected` decorator with sanic websocket route. 

Test code sample 
```python
@app.websocket('/feed2')
@protected()
async def feed(request, ws):
    await ws.send('Hello from protected websocket route')
```

This problem is caused by websocket protocol object which will travel in `args` from `is_authenticated` [method](https://github.com/ahopkins/sanic-jwt/blob/master/sanic_jwt/decorators.py#L21) to `verify` [method](https://github.com/ahopkins/sanic-jwt/blob/master/sanic_jwt/authentication.py#L153) and is mistaken for return_payload parameter

issue_title: BIP32 private key derivation edge case
issue_body:
Regarding this section of BIP32:
https://github.com/bitcoin/bips/blob/2ea19daaa0380fed7a2b053fd1f488fadba28bda/bip-0032.mediawiki#private-parent-key--private-child-key

> In case parse256(IL) ≥ n or ki = 0, the resulting key is invalid, and one should proceed with the next value for i. (Note: this has probability lower than 1 in 2^(127).)

In particular I suspect this should be implemented near:
https://github.com/richardkiss/pycoin/blob/5ebd6c038eb5de5ee5330eea70887ddd4ba390ce/pycoin/key/BIP32Node.py#L232

Alternatively, what about raising an error instead? I know this is not standards-compliant, but if someone really needs that functionality they can just increment the value themselves and try again, I believe.

issue_title: setup.py should not import pyscreeze
issue_body:
`setup.py` imports `pyscreeze` to get the version info. This then fails if the dependency `PIL` (`Pillow`) is not already installed when setup.py is run (which may be by `pip`).

Solution: put version info (and optionally any other distribution metadata) in an extra module file (e.g. `version.py`, `release.py` or `metadata.py`)  and _do not import_ it, but read it with:

```
data = {}
version_info = 'pyscreeze/version.py'
exec(compile(open(version_info).read(), version_info, 'exec'), {}, data)
version = data['__version__']
```

BTW, `from pyscreeze.version import __version__ as version` does also not work, since this will implicitly import `pyscreeze/__init__.py`.

This also prevents the possible problem where `setup.py` is run with an already installed version of pyscreeze and the version info is read from the installed package, rather from the directory, from which `setup.py` is run.

issue_title: Sometimes due to race condition PyBrowser reference lives forever
issue_body:
A global reference to PyBrowser object is kept forever and this is causing the app to not quit its message loop after windows were closed, app is still running in background or when run from shell it does not return. This is easily reproduced in hello_world.py example:

1. In google type "js alert"
2. Browse to w3schools
3. Try it - open popup
4. Close popup
5. Close main window

Now app will often not terminate its process. CEF message loop is still running. This happens because because len(g_pyBrowsers) > 0.

What happens in app is the following: a PyBrowser is created, then another PyBrowser is created, PyBrowser is closed, and then after browser was closed there is being called one of CEF handlers' callback which calls GetPyBrowser() - which creates PyBrowser again from scratch, as it was closed a moment ago. This reference now lives forever in g_pyBrowsers global list and is causing the message loop to never quit in OnBeforeClose. The solution would be to refactor GetPyBrowser to GetPyBrowserIfExists and return existing or create new PyBrowser only if it was not closed/destroyed previously. All usage of GetPyBrowser would need to be modified, so that it checks whether GetPyBrowserIfExists returned an actual object or a None value.

issue_title: No support for complex ranges
issue_body:
RFC 6020 allows ranges to be discontinuous, using the | to separate each sub-range, as long as the range delimiters increase from left to right. For example the following is allowed:

```
type int8 {
    range "-10 .. -5 | 5 .. 10 | 20 .. 30"
}
```
See section 9.2.4 for details: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6020#section-9.2.4
This also seems to apply to string lengths: https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc6020#section-9.4.4

If such a range exists in an input yang file, the follow error occurs:

```
  File "/home/msuther/Repositories/pyangbind/lib/yangtypes.py", line 839, in YANGDynClass
    return YANGBaseClass(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/msuther/Repositories/pyangbind/lib/yangtypes.py", line 717, in __init__
    raise TypeError, "couldn't generate dynamic type -> %s -> %s" % (type(e),e)
TypeError: couldn't generate dynamic type -> <type 'exceptions.ValueError'> -> too many values to unpack
```

issue_title: pytest-flake8 is incompatible with hacking module
issue_body:
I realised that adding `pytest-flake8` to you project is incompatible with use of python [hacking](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/hacking/0.12.0) package due to the conflict regarding flake8 version.

    ... flake8-3.0.4-py3.5.egg), Requirement.parse('flake8<2.6.0,>=2.5.4'), {'hacking'})

For good reasons hacking package restricts python linting packages installed to safe ranges, so people would not be surprised by their CI gates going broken.

If `pytest-flake8` would allow a wider range of flake8 version we would not risk of having to implement ugly `requirements.txt` workarounds like:
```
hacking
pytest-flake8<0.6
flake8<2.6.0
```
The last two lines are needed only because if `pytest-flake8>=0.6` would also install `flake8>3.0`.

If you have a new `pip>=9.0` you can easily find conflicts using `pip check`.

issue_title: simulate module
issue_body:
I have written a very basic simulate module, which takes an image and adds PSF blurring / noise. However, the simulate module will be a key part of AutoLens, and will requite a lot more effort!

For simulating images,  basically two things are needed:

- The code to simulate instrumental effects (e.g. read noise, poisson noise, PSF, background sky, etc.)
- An interface that allows the user to control these effects whilst not being bogged down in hard to understanding parameters.

On point 1 - The majority of effects we might want to simulate already have existing functions from libraries like AstroPy. In truth, other than the PSF, background noise and Poisson noise we probably won't want to worry about another effect for a while. I guess correlated noise due to image dithering would be the next, which goes hand-in-hand with multi image analysis and thus should be postponed until then.

On point 2 - To produce an image with appropriate signal-to-noise, I had to both change the background level of noise and the exposure time. Sometimes, the source brightness, lens light brightness and lensing magnification will also be parameters one is changing. For a user messing with parameters it becomes confusing whether the images they are producing are sensible or realistic.

When generating simulated images, users will want to interact with the code and generate images follwing two very different approaches:

- As physical as possible, using realistic magnitudes for the lens / source galaxies and realistic mass models, as well as physical parameters for the instrument. Thus, we probably want some classes which default to observed data-set results and instruments.

- Exploratory - e.g. where you ask questions like 'what does a broader PSF or lower S/N do to my results'. Here, you just want the images to come out 'looking right' but in a way you can change whatever you're interested in. For the problem above, we'd have want an automated routine that produces an image to a specified S/N.

Furthermore, it worth remember that we will want to tie the simulate module directly to the analysis modules, so one can swiftly simulate images and then see how fits behave.

issue_title: remove BOM?

issue_title: Dumping a recursion error causes a recursion error
issue_body:
Consider the following code sample:

```python
import sys
from cPickle import dumps
from tblib.pickling_support import install
install()

def f(x):
    return f(x)

try:
    f(1)
except RuntimeError:
    info = sys.exc_info()
    dumps(info[2])
```
Instead of properly storing the traceback up to the recursion limit, the exception handling raises a recursion error itself:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "crashdemo.py", line 14, in <module>
    dumps(info[2])
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/tblib/pickling_support.py", line 20, in pickle_traceback
    return unpickle_traceback, (Frame(tb.tb_frame), tb.tb_lineno, tb.tb_next and Traceback(tb.tb_next))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/tblib/__init__.py", line 63, in __init__
    self.tb_next = Traceback(tb.tb_next)
....................
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/tblib/__init__.py", line 57, in __init__
    self.tb_frame = Frame(tb.tb_frame)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/tblib/__init__.py", line 50, in __init__
    if k in ("__file__", "__name__")
RuntimeError: maximum recursion depth exceeded in cmp
```
This issue initially occured with `gevent`, as `tblib` is vendored there: https://github.com/gevent/gevent/issues/954

issue_title: Recursive Mock
issue_body:
I would like to understand how these objects could be extended to allow recursive operations. Let me explain a little of what this might look like. For example, it is often the case where

def setUp():
    session = UnifiedAlchemyMagicMock( ...)

def my_test(self):
    first_item = session.query(Blah).filter(Blah.foo == 'bar').first()
    all_items = first_item.my_relationship.filter(Foo.bar == 'blah blah').all()

I can successfully replicate using examples the first item; however I would like all objects that are subsequently passed back from the UnifiedAlchemyMagicMock() class instance to have have embedded the same mixin as the session that I originally setup. This would allow "first_item.my_relationship.filter(" type calls on those objects via the same single setup.

My ultimate goal is also to boostrap the UnifiedAlchemyMagicMock.data from a json file; that I have first streamed out of a real set of SQLAlchemy objects. This

SQL DB ---> json file --> UnifiedAlchemyMagicMock.data --> tests

This way I can decouple the SQL DB but re-use a complex objects graph that spans my data base directly in mocked tests. The code in this repo is very close to allowing the first step; but the subsequent calls to alternative relationships and association_proxy, hybrid_properties and methods needs to be ideally supported.

issue_title: KeysManager not freeing memory after use
issue_body:
With xmlsec==1.0.9, this function uses ~1MB of memory for `xmlsec.KeysManager()` on each call, that is never freed. With xmlsec==0.6.1, the memory seems to be freed when exiting the function.

```python
def _encrypt_usernametoken(self, envelope):
    """Encrypt the ``UsernameToken`` element in the ``envelope``."""
    header = envelope.find(ns(SOAP_NS, 'Header'))
    security = header.find(ns(WSSE_NS, 'Security'))

    # Create a keys manager and load the cert into it.
    manager = xmlsec.KeysManager()
    key = xmlsec.Key.from_file(self.recipient_cert, xmlsec.KeyFormat.CERT_PEM)
    manager.add_key(key)

    # Encrypt the UsernameToken
    target = security.find(ns(WSSE_NS, 'UsernameToken'))

    # Create the EncryptedData node we will replace the target node with,
    # and make sure it has the contents XMLSec expects (a CipherValue node,
    # a KeyInfo node, and an EncryptedKey node within the KeyInfo which
    # itself has a CipherValue).
    enc_data = xmlsec.template.encrypted_data_create(
        envelope,
        xmlsec.Transform.DES3,
        type=xmlsec.EncryptionType.ELEMENT,
        ns='xenc',
    )
    xmlsec.template.encrypted_data_ensure_cipher_value(enc_data)
    key_info = xmlsec.template.encrypted_data_ensure_key_info(
        enc_data, ns='dsig')
    enc_key = xmlsec.template.add_encrypted_key(
        key_info,
        # MedImpact requires RSA 1.5 encryption, which is the same as RSA
        # PKCS1.
        xmlsec.Transform.RSA_PKCS1,
    )
    xmlsec.template.encrypted_data_ensure_cipher_value(enc_key)

    enc_ctx = xmlsec.EncryptionContext(manager)
    # Generate a per-session DES key (will be encrypted using the cert).
    enc_ctx.key = xmlsec.Key.generate(
        xmlsec.KeyData.DES, 192, xmlsec.KeyDataType.SESSION)
    # Ask XMLSec to actually do the encryption.
    enc_data = enc_ctx.encrypt_xml(enc_data, target)

    # XMLSec inserts the EncryptedKey node directly within EncryptedData,
    # but WSSE wants it in the Security header instead, and referencing the
    # EncryptedData as well as the actual cert in a BinarySecurityToken.

    # Move the EncryptedKey node up into the wsse:Security header.
    security.insert(0, enc_key)

    # Create a wsse:BinarySecurityToken node containing the cert and add it
    # to the Security header.
    cert_bst = create_binary_security_token(self.recipient_cert)
    security.insert(0, cert_bst)

    # Create a ds:KeyInfo node referencing the BinarySecurityToken we just
    # created, and insert it into the EncryptedKey node.
    enc_key.insert(1, create_key_info_bst(cert_bst))

    # Add a DataReference from the EncryptedKey node to the EncryptedData.
    add_data_reference(enc_key, enc_data)

    # Remove the now-empty KeyInfo node from EncryptedData (it used to
    # contain EncryptedKey, but we moved that up into the Security header).
    enc_data.remove(key_info)
```

It also looks like our signing function leaks ~0.1MB/call with xmlsec==1.0.9:

```python
def _sign(self, envelope):
    """Sign the ``soap:Body`` and ``wsu:Timestamp`` of ``envelope``."""
    # Insert the Signature node in the wsse:Security header.
    header = envelope.find(ns(SOAP_NS, 'Header'))
    security = header.find(ns(WSSE_NS, 'Security'))

    cert_bst = create_binary_security_token(self.sender_cert)
    security.append(cert_bst)

    # Create the Signature node.
    signature = xmlsec.template.create(
        envelope,
        xmlsec.Transform.EXCL_C14N,
        xmlsec.Transform.RSA_SHA1,
    )
    key_info = create_key_info_bst(cert_bst)
    signature.append(key_info)
    security.append(signature)

    # Perform the actual signing.
    ctx = xmlsec.SignatureContext()
    ctx.key = xmlsec.Key.from_file(self.sender_key, xmlsec.KeyFormat.PEM)
    _sign_node(ctx, signature, envelope.find(ns(SOAP_NS, 'Body')))
    _sign_node(ctx, signature, security.find(ns(WSU_NS, 'Timestamp')))
    ctx.sign(signature)
```

issue_title: Stack Overflow (endless recursion) on DeviceConnected._buildPointList with NoResponseFromController
issue_body:
First off, thanks for your great library!

With at least one of my devices, I ran into a stack overflow due to an endless recursion in `/core/devices/Device.py`. It begins in `_buildPointList()` of `DeviceConnected`:

        except NoResponseFromController as error:
            self._log.error('Cannot retrieve object list, disconnecting...')
            self.segmentation_supported = False
            self.new_state(DeviceDisconnected)

The call of `new_state()` triggers line

        self.__class__ = newstate
        self._init_state()

which in turn runs `DeviceDisconnected._init_state()`:

    def _init_state(self):
        self.connect()

After a successful connection:

                if segmentation_supported:
                    self.new_state(RPMDeviceConnected)
                else:
                    self.new_state(RPDeviceConnected)

We again are in `new_state` (see above), which then calls `DeviceConnected._init_state()`:

    def _init_state(self):
        self._buildPointList()

...which closes our endless loop, since another `NoResponseFromController` exception triggers the next round.

As this is my second day working with your code, I'll refrain from posting my "solution" as a PR, as it might not be sufficient for not breaking other parts. However, it goes like this:

        except NoResponseFromController as error:
            self._log.error('Cannot retrieve object list, disconnecting...')
            self.segmentation_supported = False
            self._log.info('Changing device state to {}'.format(DeviceDisconnected))
            self.__class__ = DeviceDisconnected

issue_title: Improve error message for IA-S3 500 error's
issue_body:
Normally when IA-S3 fails, an XML formatted error message is returned in the response content. However, this is not the case for 500 errors (possibly others). Right now, `ia` simply fails with a xml.parsers.expat.ExpatError exception. This should be updated to be more clear about what's going on.

500 error's are usually either transient or require IA admin intervention to resolve. The error message should reflect this.

See #170 for an example of this issue.

   ...:         bar = C(order=1)
   ...:         baz = C(order=2)

In [6]: SomeChoices.choices
Out[6]: (('bar', 'bar'), ('baz', 'baz'), ('foo', 'foo'))
```

While we'd expect the order be the same as the class definition. This behavior is not mentioned in the docs.

issue_title: Add rate limiting algorithm
Keep containers alive after test completion
issue_body:
Sometimes it is expensive to start new containers afresh over and over again. Thus, it might be a good idea to have an option to keep the containers that were generated for the purposes of testing alive after the test is over. 

Perhaps it would be the case of including an option `keepalive` in the [container function call](https://github.com/Jc2k/pytest-docker-tools/blob/master/pytest_docker_tools/factories/container.py) and then adding it as a CLI option as well. 

If we added the option to keep containers alive, we would need to loop through the live containers to check if there is a container with the right specification and if not to start a new one. 

It might also be a good idea to allow the user, through a fixture to decide the callable to wait for. So it would be great to include `callable` as another argument.

So overall something like this:

```py
def container(request, docker_client, **kwargs):
    ''' Docker container: image={image} '''

    kwargs.update({
        'callable':  lambda: container.reload() or container.ready(),
        'keepalive': False
        'detach': True,
    })

    keepalive = kwargs['keepalive']
    callable = kwargs['callable']
    del kwargs['keepalive'], kwargs['callable']

    raw_container = None

    # loop through all existing containers
    for c in docker_client.containers.list():
        for k, v in kwargs.items():

            # was the live container initialised with the options we require?
            # each k should be available in the containers `attrs`
            if v not in c.get(k):
                raw_container = None
                break
            raw_container = c

        # if we found the right container we can proceed
        if raw_container: break

   # if the found container is sleeping, we restart it
    if raw_container and raw_container.status != 'running':
        raw_container.restart()
    # if after looping we didn't find anything, let's start a new container
    else:
        raw_container = docker_client.containers.run(**kwargs)

    # let the user decide whether to keep this container alive or not
    if not pytestconfig.getoption('keepalive'):
           request.addfinalizer(...)

    container = Container(raw_container)

    # add custom callable
    wait_for_callable(
            f'Waiting for container to be ready',
            callable
        )

    return container
```

comparing AAF and Cox
issue_body:
Hi,

first of all, thanks for this library. I am fitting both an AAF and a Cox model on my data, and I wanted to compare the results.

I noticed that the Cox predictions are always very different from the AAF ones. Once I translate everything into survival probabilities, I also compare to a plain KM measurement, and AAF agrees with KM, Cox does not.

My first explanation was that my data does not obey the multiplicative assumption and I was ready to let it go, but then I plotted the Cox baseline prediction and the "total" prediction, and they are just identical, which seem to point to the partial hazards not being properly taken into account. Is this a known bug/issue?

Any hints on how I could debug this further?

Thanks for your help,

Andrea.

issue_title: failed trying Telegram Webhook (CPython) with letsencrypt's certificate
issue_body:
Please answer these questions before submitting your issue. Thanks!

1. What version of pyTelegramBotAPI are you using?
2.2.3 (latest)

2. What OS are you using?
Debian GNU/Linux 8

3. What version of python are you using?
Python 2.7.9

Hi!
I'm using Telegram webhook example with **CPython** and **letsencrypt's certificate**. I test my bot with:

`wget -O /dev/null https://mydomain.net:8443/`

It works, and my bot can notify me when a do wget:

 `[02/Feb/2017 17:28:21] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 -`

But when i try to send a simple message to my bot through Telegram, it doesn't work and my bot prints:
```
[02/Feb/2017 17:23:34] code 403, message Forbidden
[02/Feb/2017 17:23:34] "POST / HTTP/1.1" 403 -
```

I started with self certificate and it works. With letsencrypt's certificate no.
This is my bot code:

```
#!/usr/bin/python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

# This is a simple echo bot using decorators and webhook with BaseHTTPServer
# It echoes any incoming text messages and does not use the polling method.

import BaseHTTPServer
import ssl
import telebot
import logging


API_TOKEN = 'MYTOKEN'

WEBHOOK_HOST = 'mydomain.net'
WEBHOOK_PORT = 8443  # 443, 80, 88 or 8443 (port need to be 'open')
WEBHOOK_LISTEN = '0.0.0.0'  # In some VPS you may need to put here the IP addr

WEBHOOK_SSL_CERT = '/etc/letsencrypt/live/mydomain.net/fullchain.pem'  # Path to the ssl certificate
WEBHOOK_SSL_PRIV = '/etc/letsencrypt/live/mydomain.net/privkey.pem'  # Path to the ssl private key

##SELF works
#WEBHOOK_SSL_CERT = '/etc/opt/webhook_cert.pem'  # Path to the ssl certificate
#WEBHOOK_SSL_PRIV = '/etc/opt/webhook_pkey.pem'  # Path to the ssl private key

WEBHOOK_URL_BASE = "https://%s:%s" % (WEBHOOK_HOST, WEBHOOK_PORT)
WEBHOOK_URL_PATH = "/%s/" % (API_TOKEN)


logger = telebot.logger
telebot.logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

bot = telebot.TeleBot(API_TOKEN)


# WebhookHandler, process webhook calls
class WebhookHandler(BaseHTTPServer.BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    server_version = "WebhookHandler/1.0"

    def do_HEAD(self):
        self.send_response(200)
        self.end_headers()

    def do_GET(self):
        self.send_response(200)
        self.end_headers()

    def do_POST(self):
        if self.path == WEBHOOK_URL_PATH and \
           'content-type' in self.headers and \
           'content-length' in self.headers and \
           self.headers['content-type'] == 'application/json':
            json_string = self.rfile.read(int(self.headers['content-length']))

            self.send_response(200)
            self.end_headers()

            update = telebot.types.Update.de_json(json_string)
            bot.process_new_messages([update.message])
        else:
            self.send_error(403)
            self.end_headers()


# Handle '/start' and '/help'
@bot.message_handler(commands=['help', 'start'])
def send_welcome(message):
    bot.reply_to(message,
                 ("Hi there, I am EchoBot.\n"
                  "I am here to echo your kind words back to you."))


# Handle all other messages
@bot.message_handler(func=lambda message: True, content_types=['text'])
def echo_message(message):
    bot.reply_to(message, message.chat.id)

# Remove webhook, it fails sometimes the set if there is a previous webhook
bot.remove_webhook()

# Set webhook
bot.set_webhook(url=WEBHOOK_URL_BASE+WEBHOOK_URL_PATH,
                certificate=open(WEBHOOK_SSL_CERT, 'r'))

# Start server
httpd = BaseHTTPServer.HTTPServer((WEBHOOK_LISTEN, WEBHOOK_PORT),
                                  WebhookHandler)

httpd.socket = ssl.wrap_socket(httpd.socket,
                               certfile=WEBHOOK_SSL_CERT,
                               keyfile=WEBHOOK_SSL_PRIV,
                               server_side=True)

httpd.serve_forever()

```

Thank you very much in advance.

issue_title: _max_results issue
issue_body:
When _max_results is used with other parameters using '&', the WAPI call does not return and it gets timed out.

To simulate this from CURL, the following will have the same effect.

curl -H "Content-Type: application/json" -k1 -u cloud:cloud -X GET https://10.39.12.170/wapi/v2.3/networkview?_return_fields=name,is_default,extattrs&_max_results=-10

This is because '&' is used as background process in command line.

In order to fix this issue, double-quotes around URL is needed. So this works.

curl -H "Content-Type: application/json" -k1 -u cloud:cloud -X GET "https://10.39.12.170/wapi/v2.3/networkview?_return_fields=name,is_default,extattrs&_max_results=-10"

Surprisingly, connector.py has the same issue.

Try to put double-quotes around the URL.
Double-quotes are better than single-quotes.

issue_title: Filtering with booleans
issue_body:
Hello @sileht, thanks for sharing this project.

I've been using jsonpath-rw for a while and now needed to do some filtering so I ended up finding jsonpath-rw-ext which seems to match exactly what I'm looking for.

However I got stuck at the point where I tried filtering based on boolean values in the data. It doesn't seem to work...

Example:

    import jsonpath_rw_ext

    data = [{"color": "blue", "flag": True}, {"color": "green", "flag": False}]
    jsp = jsonpath_rw_ext.parse("$[?flag = True].color")
    results = [match.value for match in jsp.find(data)]
    print(results)

I expected this to produce `results == ["blue"]` but got `[]` instead.

I digged in a little bit and it seems to comes down to the fact that the `True` in the `flag = True` expression ends up as a string `"True"` in the `Expression` instance `self.value` in _filters.py (instead of ending up as a boolean `True`). Digging further, points to a limitation in the lexer/parser in jsonpath-rw codebase.

Questions:
* Do you have any suggestion for a workaround that does not involve pre-processing the data?
* Do you think a fix for this could be made at the jsonpath-rw-ext level or would it need to go into jsonpath-rw's codebase?
* Lastly: you mention that these extensions would be proposed to be integrated into jsonpath-rw. Any progress on that?

Again thanks for sharing this code and for any feedback you may share.

issue_title: torchfile.load() returns empty array on torch.ByteTensor
issue_body:
Disclaimer: new to trying to work with serialized data, so this may be user error.

I've been working through implementing Professor de Freitas's [Machine Learning course materials](https://www.cs.ox.ac.uk/people/nando.defreitas/machinelearning/) in Tensorflow ([here](https://github.com/jakedailey1/OxfordML-NDF)) and looking to use the data [here](http://www.cs.ox.ac.uk/people/brendan.shillingford/teaching/practical-data.tar.gz) to write the LSTM from Practical 6...I was delighted to find that you'd already gone to the trouble of writing this package!

`torchfile.load()` works fine for vocab.t7, but returns an empty array when I try running `torchfile.load('train.t7')`. When I dig into the code deeper, it seems that `T7Reader` correctly finds that `typeidx == 4` (Torch) after calling reader.read_obj() a first time. 

The code proceeds to call `type_handlers` but then on the second call to `read_obj()` within `read_tensor_generic`, finds that typeidx==0. The result is that `storage is None`, causing the code to return an empty array. 

For reference, the `class_name` found is torch.ByteTensor and the `version_number` returned is 1. I'm running Python 3.5 via Conda on Windows x64. 

Thank you!
Jake

issue_title: Problem with custom CSRF cookie name
Sharing session context / Repository pattern
issue_body:
Hi,

For my project we heavily use the repository pattern to abstract database queries. These repositories use the session to connect to the database, and are usually injected into the handlers. 

The easiest solution to make sure the repositories use the correct session is to create them within the context of the request. So something like this. 

```class ExamplerHandler(JsonHandler, SessionMixin):
    def get(self, testId):
        with self.make_session() as session:
            self.repository = TestRepository(session)
```

However, I think it would be a better separation of concerns if the handlers aren't tasked with object creation themselves. In order to do this it would be necessary to somehow share the current context between the handlers and other objects (e.g. the repository). I think this might be possible using e.g. Tornado's stack context (see [Context local](https://github.com/viewfinderco/viewfinder/blob/master/backend/base/context_local.py) for an examle).

What are your thoughts on this?

issue_title: Enable handling ToS text fields as template
issue_body:
Hi,
I came across a use case, where a ToS text has to include other variables and e.g. urls defined by `{% url 'my-url-name' %}`. In general, the ToS text (or other field) could contain template-like syntax.

This could be done by including a `terms.text` in a form of a Template object and using the `include` tag. Thus, one would need to customize the respective template file and use there `{% include terms.text|as_template %}` instead of `{{ terms.text|safe }}`.
I can do a PR with an additional template tag, and help info. Let me know if you think this makes sense.

Hi - 

Great work on the python-edtf implementation. I'm hoping to use and contribute to this project for a larger application that stores fragmentary and ambiguous dates. 

Can you explain your thinking around the EDTFDate.precision function? I do have some confusion around what's being defined as "precision".

issue_title: Add number of rows to table common properties upon writing
issue_body: Since iterating over rows of a CLDF table may be costly (due to validation/conversion), one may wish to avoid it when a simple row count is needed. As a shortcut, pycldf should add the number of rows as common property `dc:extent` to the table when writing rows.

issue_title: `deis run` hangs on failed container
issue_body:
If you do a `deis run` and the container `fail`s, the command just hangs with no response, and never cleans up the scheduled admin container in fleet.

for example, before PR'ing https://github.com/deis/deis/pull/2642, I was testing with https://github.com/deis/example-dockerfile-http which does not have `bash` present in the image (we use that as the entrypoint for run commands):

```
><> deis run echo hi
running 'echo hi'...
^C
><> fleetctl list-units | grep dock_v2.run.1
dock_v2.run.1.service		2280c10f.../10.21.12.2	failed		failed
```

This is not a regression that was recently introduced, just a bug that's been there for a while.

issue_title: Erroneous Error on Exec Depends That are Also Test Depends
issue_body:
I have found that if I find_package() a dependency for unit tests (in my case, roslaunch), catkin_lint will find an error unless the dependency is listed as a test_depend or a build_depend. Listing the package as an exec_depend does not fulfill the requirement for catkin_lint.

According to http://wiki.ros.org/catkin/package.xml, "Test Dependencies specify only additional dependencies for unit tests. They should never duplicate any dependencies already mentioned as build or run dependencies. " It seems that specifying the package as an exec_depend fulfills this requirement, so catkin_lint shouldn't consider this an error.

issue_title: Race condition with observers
issue_body:
The way that we use observers on the AllWatcher to watch for expected changes in the model is racey.  In most (all?) cases where we use observers, we request a change via the API and then subsequently register an observer to watch for that change to be realized on the model.  However, depending on load and how quickly the model can be updated, the notification on the AllWatcher could come in before the initial API call response is returned and/or handled via async.

issue_title: pipenv uses deprecated pypi.python.org
issue_body: pypi.python.org is deprecated in favor of pypi.org. Most tools have updated and the default is to use https://pypi.org/ as the default repository. A [quick search](https://github.com/pypa/pipenv/search?utf8=%E2%9C%93&q=%22pypi.python.org%22&type=Code) reveals 33 cases where pipenv uses pypi.python.org. It should probably instead use pypi.org instead.

issue_title: Could pipfile be designed to support a MetaPathFinder implementation that installs the requirements 'on-demand' ?
issue_body:
I learned about `MetaPathFinder` class from importlib and created this hack:

http://lonetwin.github.io/blog/html/2016/05/16/auto_install_missing_python_modules.html

Since I discovered this hack, I have been thinking that something of an 'install-on-missing-import' feature would be, in theory, a possibility,

I've been wrestling with getting an implementation which works with requirements.txt for and in fact, I do have a rough implementation which parses the requirements.txt and installs the requirements at import time. This works for most simple installations, although I haven't tested it extensively.

I would like to suggest that maybe `pipfile` could designed (and possibly provide a reference implementation) of a `MetaPathFinder` which ensures that the developers and users of modules don't even have to worry about running a command to install anything. Just executing or importing the module should install the necessary dependencies. 

This is just a suggestion and I'm ok if people think this idea is ridiculous. That said, knowing the reasons why such a thing would be ridiculous would be appreciated.

issue_title: @Import is broken?
issue_body:
Installation was successful with 'npm install bootstrap-sass', but when I try to @import 'bootstrap', I'm told that it cannot be found.

>Module build failed: Error: C:\repos\vso\AxisCodeWeb\src\components\App\App.scss:16:1: Failed to find 'bootstrap'

I tried changing to @import 'bootstrap-sass', and this also does not work.  

>Module build failed: Error: C:\repos\vso\AxisCodeWeb\src\components\App\App.scss:16:1: Failed to find 'bootstrap-sass'

I can make slight progress by trying 'npm install bootstrap' and using @import 'bootstrap', but that is not a dependency of bootstrap-sass and it means I am no longer using bootstrap-sass.  

The context of my problem is that I'm trying to [incorporate bootstrap into the react-starter-kit](https://github.com/kriasoft/react-starter-kit/issues/179).  In that discussion they don't bother with bootstrap-sass, which makes no sense to me.  The react starter kit uses SASS, and so bootstrap-sass seems like the right choice.

issue_title: Consider changing our `block` terminology
issue_body:
I propose changing `taskBlock` and `nodes` to `tasks_per_node` and `nodes_per_block`. Instead of users having to learn: 
- Block concept
- Node concept
- Node parameter definition (Node parameter = Nodes / Block)
- TaskBlock concept
- TaskBlock parameter definition (TaskBlock parameter = TaskBlocks / Block)
- Task concept

They would just have to learn:
- Block concept
- Node concept
- Task concept

The parameter definitions would be clear from the name. For example, with the current terminology, you would need to look at the illustrations in the docs to understand the definition. On the other hand, I think most people could draw the corresponding diagram given the names `tasks_per_node` and `nodes_per_block`.

Example 1
----------
**current**
```
"block": {
    "taskBlocks": 4,
    "nodes": 1
}
```

**proposed**
```
"block": {
    "nodes_per_block": 1,
    "tasks_per_node": 4
}
```
![n1_t4](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5114833/40025382-ff02696c-5796-11e8-9d42-abccd6803ba5.png)


Example 2
----------

**current**
```
"block": {
    "taskBlocks": 2,
    "nodes": 4
}
```

**proposed**
```
"block": {
    "nodes_per_block": 4,
    "tasks_per_node": 1 / 2.
}
```
![n4_t2](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5114833/40025377-fd62435c-5796-11e8-98d6-281d5661a59d.png)

issue_title: Data Management, representation and implementation
issue_body:
We are moving away from a config definition that is used by the DFK to initialize resources to a model in which the user uses a `Config` class that takes initialized resources as parameters. This discussion is here: https://github.com/Parsl/parsl/issues/133. Each executor in this model represents an elastic pool of compute resources. How data assets/methods should map to this model is unclear given only preliminary work has been done on the representation of data management in our current static config based system.
 
Some terminology
1. We have the concept of a `DataManager(DM)` which is an executor private to the DFK. The DM is simply responsible for moving files
2. An `endpoint(EP)` is a block of attributes that define the transfer methods and details like filepaths, ports, auth.
3. An `executor` represents a collection of resources gathered from a computation site. For a single computation site like NERSC you could have several executors that handle specific queues, node requirements etc.

The DM assumes that it can move files between any pair of EPs. Since the DM is generally hidden from the user one possibility is that it can be a default kwarg like this : 
`Config(data_manager=DataManager(threads=4), .... )`

If the EP definition naturally matches to the notion of an executor, the new class structure and our implementation of the executors should be updated to match that. I do not know if this is the case.

<Put picture here>

issue_title: Usage tracking with certain missing network causes 20s startup delay.
issue_body:
Usage tracking appears to happen synchronously when something like this is called:

    dfk = parsl.DataFlowKernel(executors=[workers])

When something is wrong with the network that causes long delays (for example, slow/timing-out name resolution - for example, in a container with a weird network configuration), startup of the DataFlowKernel is delayed, before the program then successfully continues onwards.

In my case, I'm running in a docker container where the `/etc/resolv.conf` resolver points to a not-responding IP address, and the startup delay is 20 seconds.

No log message appears to be output describing the cause of the delay.

This occurs with several versions, including 48d46ba04afcd86c984953c1d75b7818101cf87f

issue_title: `conda reload` command
issue_body:
I often find myself needing to re-load the current environment, using `source activate <env>`, for example after rebuilding and re-installing a package that I'm working on. It would be helpful to have an env-agnostic command that could be called from a rebuild script, like this:

```
conda build <recipe>
conda uninstall <package>
conda install <package> --use-local
conda reload
```

where `conda reload` basically does `source activate <current-env>`, without having to specify the env name.

issue_title: Solver improvements: 3) store clauses in a more memory efficient way
issue_body:
ref: https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/7676#issuecomment-415810372
> - Decide whether we should/can use `ClauseArray` instead of `ClauseList`
>   - Goal: Reduce memory.
>   - Goal: Avoid costly processing of list of tuple of ints in SAT solver's Python interface.
>   - Requirement: Try to make it faster so it's not *that much* slower than `ClauseList`.
>   - Alternative: See if we can avoid storing clauses at all in the Python part. refs: https://github.com/msoos/cryptominisat/issues/513#issuecomment-415503020 and https://github.com/msoos/cryptominisat/issues/513#issuecomment-415505531

If we implement support for reading clauses as provided by `ClauseArray.as_array()` in Pycosat and CryptoMiniSat (https://github.com/msoos/cryptominisat/pull/515), this can save a good chunk of memory, see https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/7676#issuecomment-414338896 and also make passing the clauses to the SAT solvers faster since they wouldn't have to unpack all those Python tuples.
That speed improvement is currently negated by the fact that adding clauses to a `ClauseArray` instance is slower than adding them to a `ClauseList` instance. If we translate the part of the code where the majority of clauses are added to compiled code, we can avoid that slowness and would even be much faster than when `ClauseList` is used, xref: https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/7696.

Additionally, it makes very much sense to fuse the `ClauseArray` (`ClauseList`) and `SatSolver` classes which have been introduced in https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/7676. That way we could easily use a uniform interface for adding clauses as well as restoring the solver state. So we might even get away with not storing the clauses at all on our part but potentially just let the SAT solvers do all of that for us. Very good suggestions for strategies to rollback the solver state are given by @msoos in https://github.com/msoos/cryptominisat/issues/513#issuecomment-415503020 ff.

issue_title: Defaults channel has incorrect repodata
issue_body:
Take a look at https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main/win-64/repodata.json

As an example, let's look at python-2.7.13-1.tar.bz2

In this channel, this package has the following repodata:

"python-2.7.13-1.tar.bz2": {
      "build": "1",
      "build_number": 1,
      "date": "2017-05-11",
      "depends": [
        "vc 9.*",
        "vs2008_runtime"
      ],
      "license": "PSF",
      "md5": "fd115c17839a0aad86f63f4143577d07",
      "name": "python",
      "size": 24990162,
      "space_anchor": true,
      "version": "2.7.13"
    },

However, if you download the package and use "conda index" (even with latest conda: 4.3.30) on a local channel, the resulting repodata is as follows:

"python-2.7.13-1.tar.bz2": {
      "build": "1",
      "build_number": 1,
      "depends": [
        "vs2008_runtime"
      ],
      "license": "PSF",
      "md5": "fd115c17839a0aad86f63f4143577d07",
      "name": "python",
      "sha256": "d841a927a934f9394ff133c94e9fbc59fb77b3b11dc859ef1220f999202963c6",
      "sig": null,
      "size": 24990162,
      "subdir": "win-64",
      "timestamp": 1509559249,
      "track_features": "vc9",
      "version": "2.7.13"
    },

This dependency mismatch breaks the fundamental assumption that if you use a particular version of a package from a particular channel, it will pull in a known, non-changing set of dependencies. If this is a bug fix, it should only affect new packages going forward. The repodata and dependencies of old packages should never change.

issue_title: portal.get_current_language does not negotiate for language variants
issue_body:
Debugging this with @mauritsvanrees this afternoon:

portal.translate depends on portal.get_current_language to pass the current language to the translate function.  get_current_language its docstring says   """Return the current negotiated language.""", but it does not do any negotiation as it is provided by zope.i18n.negotiate. 

This causes Plone sites with a configured language variante like nl-be (Flemish), not to fallback to 'nl' for fetching the Dutch translation.  Although portal.translate eventually calls zope.i18n.translate, the context is no longer available and the target_language is set to 'nl-be', for which no .po files can be found. zope.i18n.translate does not call negotiate if target_language is set explicitly and no context is found.

Proposed solution is to call zope.i18n's negotiate function, this would do what the docstring promises. But meanwhile other code might depend on get_current_language fetching the actual language on the request, which may or may not be a variant. 

So maybe portal.translate should not use get_current_language but call a new get_current_translation_language

A related feature could be to allow passing in the request object into the translate function, then it is not necessary to set target_language. 

@ebrehault @jensens @rnixx You've been working on these plone.api methods in the past and have some experience with this, is the above a correct analysis?

issue_title: overflow error in v_from_i for some conditions
issue_body:
PR #190 replaced a numerical solution for v_oc with an analytical solution. @cwhanse warned that the analytical solution is prone to numerical overflow for modules with large Voc. Cliff's suggested test passed, though, so I figured that we were in good shape and the difference could be attributed to something about Python vs. Matlab. Well, I found a situation where the analytical solution does fail...

```python
In [76]:     module_parameters = modules['Canadian_Solar_CS5P_220M'].copy()
    ...:     module_parameters['EgRef'] = 1.121
    ...:     module_parameters['dEgdT'] = -0.0002677

In [77]: IL, I0, Rs, Rsh, nNsVth = pvlib.pvsystem.calcparams_desoto(1000, 30, module_parameters['alpha_sc'], module_parameters, module_parameters['EgRef'], module_parameters['dEgdT'])

In [78]: IL, I0, Rs, Rsh, nNsVth
Out[78]: (5.1366949999999996, 1.8739027472625636e-09, 1.065, 381.68, 2.681527737715915)

In [79]: I = 0

# the line in v_from_i that fails
In [80]: I0 * Rsh / nNsVth * np.exp(Rsh * (-I + IL + I0) / nNsVth)
Out[80]: inf
```

Following pvlib matlab's v_from_i, we can calculate the lambertw term in log space...

```python
In [81]:     logargW = (np.log(I0) + np.log(Rsh) - np.log(nNsVth) +
    ...:                Rsh * (-I + IL + I0) / nNsVth)

In [82]:     w = logargW
    ...:     for i in range(0, 3):
    ...:         w = w * (1 - np.log(w) + logargW) / (1 + w)
    ...:     lambertwterm_log = w
    ...:

In [83]: lambertwterm_log
Out[83]: 709.43905979862768
```

I'll put this in a PR soon.

issue_title: Call symbol/nd.smooth_l1 without scalar causing uncaught exception
issue_body:
Call mx.nd.smooth_l1 without scalar parameter will cause mxnet to crash
```
libc++abi.dylib: terminating with uncaught exception of type std::invalid_argument: stod: no conversion
```

## Steps to reproduce
or if you are running standard examples, please provide the commands you have run that lead to the error.

```
import mxnet.ndarray as nd
nd.smooth_l1(nd.array([1, 2]))
```

## What have you tried to solve it?

Specify scalar will solve it
```
nd.smooth_l1(nd.array([1, 2]), scalar=1.0)
```
However, in the example, the argument is specified as `sigma`, which is very confusing.
http://mxnet.io/api/python/symbol.html#mxnet.symbol.smooth_l1

We should add a default value for scalar (1.0), fix the document, and fix the uncaught exception anyway

import os

from data import mnist_iterator
import mxnet as mx
import logging

class perturbation(mx.operator.CustomOp):
    def __init__(self, ctx):
        super(perturbation, self).__init__()       
        self.rand = None 
        self.ctx = ctx
        
    def forward(self, is_train, req, in_data, out_data, aux):
        x = in_data[0]
        self.rand = mx.random.normal(loc=1, scale=1,shape=x.shape[0], ctx=self.ctx)
        y = x*self.rand
        self.assign(out_data[0], req[0], y)

    def backward(self, req, out_grad, in_data, out_data, in_grad, aux):
        y = self.rand*out_grad
        self.assign(in_grad[0], req[0], y)
        
@mx.operator.register("perturbation")
class perturbationProp(mx.operator.CustomOpProp):

    def list_arguments(self):

    def list_outputs(self):

    def infer_shape(self, in_shape):
        data_shape = in_shape[0]
        output_shape = in_shape[0]
        return [data_shape], [output_shape], []

    def create_operator(self, ctx, shapes, dtypes):

fc2 = mx.symbol.FullyConnected(data = act1, name = 'fc2', num_hidden = 64)

# train

logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)

# MXNET_CPU_WORKER_NTHREADS must be greater than 1 for custom op to work on CPU
model = mx.model.FeedForward(
    ctx = mx.cpu(0), symbol = mlp, num_epoch = 10,
    learning_rate = 0.1, momentum = 0.9, wd = 0.00001)

model.fit(X=train, eval_data=val,
          batch_end_callback=mx.callback.Speedometer(100,100))

```

issue_title: nd.softmax() doesn't support grad_req='add'
issue_body:
## Description
`nd.softmax()` does not support adding gradients specified by setting `grad_req` to `'add'`. This is obvious in [this line of code](https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/a0a52b38a9046a3ede20c900ff64154642e8e2da/src/operator/nn/softmax-inl.h#L267). 
However, `nd.SoftmaxActivation` which is deprecated does support `grad_req` equal to `'add'`. This issue came up in [this discuss questions](https://discuss.mxnet.io/t/aggregate-gradients-manually-over-n-batches/504/14?u=safrooze).

Package used: Python

## Error Message:
```
mxnet.base.MXNetError: [18:24:22] src/operator/nn/./softmax-inl.h:267: Check failed: req[0] != kAddTo (3 vs. 3)
```

## Minimum reproducible example
```python
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon, nd, autograd

print(mx.__version__)

grad_req = 'add'

x = (nd.random.uniform(shape=(2, 10)) - 0.5) * 10
x_g = nd.zeros_like(x)

autograd.mark_variables(x, x_g, grad_req)

with autograd.record():
    # Change nd.softmax to nd.SoftmaxActivation and the code doesn't throw an error
    y = nd.softmax(x)
y.backward()
print(x_g.asnumpy())
```

issue_title: CancellableStream: AttributeError: module 'urllib3.contrib' has no attribute 'pyopenssl'
issue_body:
Hi there 👋,

It looks like 40711cb50189c4c39ed5a60c16910646a00f9acc introduced code that requires that `docker` is installed with the `tls` extra: https://github.com/docker/docker-py/blob/1e389b7cfe12eb04685d331a640cd8dac77206d1/docker/types/daemon.py#L60-L61

The `urllib3.contrib.pyopenssl` package is only available if the `tls` extra requirements are installed and I get an error like `AttributeError: module 'urllib3.contrib' has no attribute 'pyopenssl'` otherwise.

I don't _think_ it's necessary to install the `tls` extras for non-Windows, non-Python 2 platforms to have working HTTPS and it doesn't seem intentional that this is a requirement to use streams.

I'll try write a small PR to fix this.

issue_title: Add include/exclue fields in ElasticDocument GetSource
Expand `globus login` to do a full 3-legged OAuth Flow
issue_body:
There will be general-purpose support for public clients in Globus Auth in the near future.
At that time, we want `globus login` to use a public client registration to start a 3-legged flow with the following basic usage pattern:

- `globus login` displays a link to copy-paste into a browser window
- Globus Auth consumes that link, does a 3-legged flow, and displays for copy-paste an authorization code
- `globus login` (still open) consumes that authorization code as an input and exchanges it for refresh tokens, which are saved
- Print instructions for testing that your tokens work

This requires that the SDK changes its token handling to be ready for refresh tokens, and that the support in Globus Auth is completed.

issue_title: Look into moving JWT support to PyJWT; consider whether or not we can make it a strict dependency (vs. optional)
issue_body:
For JWT support, we allow users to install python-jose via the `globus-sdk[jwt]` extra. That makes the inclusion optional.
We made it optional because python-jose depends on pycrypto, which doesn't install cleanly without system libraries.

pyjwt is more mainstream than python-jose, and depends on `cryptography`. Among the many nice things about `cryptography` over `pycrypto`, it ships statically linked wheels for many platforms.

This means that a transition could do a couple of things for us:
- Improve the security of the tools we're implicitly endorsing, moving from `pycrypto` (which is dead) to `cryptography`
- Use deps more aligned with the CLI (which requires `cryptography` for delegate proxy)
- Possibly turn `jwt` support on by default, making `cryptography` a required dependency
  - This might work on our target platforms due to the static wheels as a format
  - We would need to expand our installation documentation to include notes on installing on the rare, unsupported platforms for `cryptography`
    e.g. `sudo apt-get install build-essential libssl-dev libffi-dev python3-dev`

However, this inherently makes `globus-sdk` installation more prone to failure. We're relying upon `cryptography` to retain good support for our target platforms, and if a platform is missing we expose a lot of extra setup burden.
We need to be *very confident* before pursuing this.

issue_title: stripe.api_requestor._api_encode ambiguously encodes list of dicts
issue_body:
I'm filing this bug to follow up on a support conversation with Thomas and Armand, who were able to reproduce this issue.

I dug into how the request arguments are converted, and it seems stripe.api_requestor._api_encode encodes the items in an ambiguous way:

```
>>> items = [{'quantity': 1, 'parent': 'black', 'type': 'sku'}, {'description': 'Gift credit', 'type': 'discount', 'amount': -3900, 'currency': 'usd'}] 
>>> list(stripe.api_requestor._api_encode({"items": items}))
[('items[][quantity]', 1), ('items[][type]', 'sku'), ('items[][parent]', 'black'), ('items[][type]', 'discount'), ('items[][description]', 'Gift credit'), ('items[][currency]', 'usd'), ('items[][amount]', -3900)]
```

This can result in the Python library submitting incorrect data to the API.

From Armand:

> Armand here, jumping in for Thomas, I hope you don't mind! I've spent some time investigating the issue and I believe you're correct: lists of hashes are encoded in an ambiguous way. This, coupled with the fact that Python hashes (dicts) are unordered, can result in inconsistent behavior.
> 
> One possible workaround would be to use OrderedMap and make sure to insert the `type` attribute first in each item's dict. On the API's end, when a key appears twice in the same list, it will be interpreted as a new element in the list.
> 
> Using OrderedMaps is a bit unwieldy though, so a simpler workaround is to replace the list by a hash with incrementing numerical keys, e.g.:
> 
> ```
> >>> items = {0: {'quantity': 1, 'parent': 'black', 'type': 'sku'}, 1: {'description': 'Gift credit', 'type': 'discount', 'amount': -3900, 'currency': 'usd'}}
> >>> list(stripe.api_requestor._api_encode({"items": items}))
> [('items[1][amount]', -3900), ('items[1][description]', 'Gift credit'), ('items[1][type]', 'discount'), ('items[1][currency]', 'usd'), ('items[0][quantity]', 1), ('items[0][parent]', 'black'), ('items[0][type]', 'sku')]
> ```
> 
> This removes the ambiguity and will be decoded correctly on the API's end.
> 
> In any case, this is probably something that should be handled in the bindings themselves. I will forward the details to the bindings maintainers and discuss the best way to fix this issue.

issue_title: Is there a way for conditional inclusion of yaml configuration?
issue_body:
Right now we need to put a configuration file for all the machines that we would like to use the sos remote execution feature. This can be inconvenient if we would like to change configuration for a server. Is there a way for us to create a single configuration file that can be used for all machines? I do not mind borrow strength from `.bashrc` etc as long as we only to maintain a single copy of configuration file for all machines.

If this is not possible, for example, because we have to define `path_map` for individual machine, perhaps we can allow the definition of multiple machines in one file? Something like:

```
hostname1:
    server1:
          config
hostname2:
     server1:
           config
```

Or we can separate our server configurations by

```
remote_server:
    server1:
         server_config
         home: 
local_host:
    host1:
         home:
    host2:
         home
```

and then we know how to map from `host1` and `host2` to `remote_server`.

issue_title: RELICENSE pyzmq core
issue_body:
Following libzmq (https://github.com/zeromq/libzmq/issues/2376), I'd like to attempt to relicense away from LGPL. That issue is largely copied here, with small substitutions for BSD instead of MPLv2, but MPLv2 is left as an option.

Unlike libzmq itself, the preferred new license for pyzmq is [3-Clause BSD](https://opensource.org/licenses/BSD-3-Clause) because the majority of pyzmq is already licensed under BSD. Only the low-level Cython bindings are licensed under LGPL as a historical artifact, so only those who have contributed to that part of pyzmq (zmq.backend.cython, zmq.core, zmq._zmq, depending on the point in history) are required to submit grants in order for this process to complete.

We are requesting contributors to send in a license grant to relicense pyzmq contributions to one of the options listed below:

1. relicense to the MPLv2 or any Open Source Initiative (OSI) approved license chosen by the current ZeroMQ BDFL **(preferred selection)**
1. relicense to the BSD license specifically
1. relicense to the MPLv2 license specifically (only choose this if you object to permissive licenses)

If a contribution was made during working hours for an employer then we may also need a license grant (or "quit claim") from the employer.

We would like to follow a certain template as license grant to ensure all the necessary information is included.  There are three templates, one for each option.  Please choose the template that best represents your wishes.

The templates can be found at: https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/tree/master/RELICENSE/templates

To include the license grant, please:
- fork the original pyzmq repository
- create a new file in the RELICENSE directory, named after your name and/or your employers name, with the license grant as content, using the template chosen
- commit this file to the repository, using the same email address as your earlier contributions
- create a pull request on pyzmq to have your license grant merged into the repository.

See the existing files in:
https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/tree/master/RELICENSE

or this specific example: https://github.com/zeromq/pyzmq/blob/master/RELICENSE/minrk.md

for a guide for how to fill out the template.

---

If there are any questions, don't hesitate to ask them below.

issue_title: Training loops
issue_body:
So far the only thing that's been implemented is a bunch of tools to generate Theano expressions for neural nets. There is no actual training code in the library yet.

We should provide some premade 'training loops' for common use cases, that take care of things like compiling the necessary Theano functions and updating the parameters given a dataset.

It would be great if we could rely on Python generators for this - although at this point I'm not sure if they offer enough flexibility. But if they do, it would be great to be able to avoid adding another class / abstraction.

We could provide a few different types of training loops, for different dataset sizes and approaches. For example, some datasets fit into GPU memory, so we should provide a loop that loads up the data into a shared variable and then iterates over that in batches. But a lot of datasets actually don't, so then we'd have to load a new 'chunk' of data into the shared variable at regular intervals.

Until now I've always reimplemented this type of thing specifically for the problems I was working on (layers.py only provided tools to generate Theano expressions, nothing else). But I've definitely felt like I was reinventing the wheel a couple of times :)

I don't have a concrete idea yet of how we should implement this, input is very welcome.

issue_title: RFC: Unify conv/pool layers
issue_body:
It has come up a couple of times as I have introduced people (cc @bmcfee, @moscow25, and issue #128) to Lasagne that someone is trying to use some code which uses, for example, `Conv2DDNNLayer` on a machine which has no GPU or does not have cudnn installed (this can happen when you're running someone else's code, or when you want to test out some code you wrote on your GPU box on your GPU-less laptop).  The simple fix is generally to just use `Conv2DLayer` instead, but it's not really a portable solution to tell people to modify code when it's being used on different systems.  A simple hack fix would be for all scripts which use Lasagne's layers which have optional dependencies to have something like this (as an example) at the top:
```Python
try:
    import lasagne.layers.dnn
    conv_layer = lasagne.layers.dnn.Conv2DNNLayer
except ImportError:
    conv_layer = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer
...
# All convolutional layers would then be instantiated like this
layer = conv_layer(incoming, ...)
```
I don't really think it's fair to encourage all of our users to do something this hacky, though, and I think we owe it to users to abstract this out.  To me, a good solution would be for there to be a single `Conv2DLayer` which had an additional kwarg such as `backend`, which could be, say, `'theano'`, `'cuda_convnet'`, `'dnn'`, etc., which chose which backend to use.  Given that the different backends have different requirements, we'd need to be diligent (as we already are) about throwing errors when a backend can't support the provided layer arguments.  The simplest way to implement this would be to basically just make `Conv2DLayer` a wrapper class around all of these layers, which threw warnings when it fell back on the theano backend because of something not being present.  I believe this also applies to pooling layers.  If and when theano does this for us, we can greatly simplify our code, but I think it's worth abstracting this out now instead of waiting.  I don't have enough in-depth knowledge of the different layers/backends to know if there is something making this not possible, so please let me know if there is.

issue_title: "Buffer overflow in broker connection" upon SimpleConsumer init
issue_body:
I'm reading from a slightly synthetic topic here, with thousands of 1kB messages - still, not outlandish in size. Starting a consumer for that topic, I'm seeing
```
Exception in thread Thread-12:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/threading.py", line 810, in __bootstrap_inner
    self.run()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/threading.py", line 763, in run
    self.__target(*self.__args, **self.__kwargs)
  File "pykafka/simpleconsumer.py", line 192, in fetcher
    self.fetch()
  File "pykafka/simpleconsumer.py", line 349, in fetch
    min_bytes=self._fetch_min_bytes
  File "pykafka/broker.py", line 209, in fetch_messages
    return future.get(FetchResponse)
  File "pykafka/handlers.py", line 50, in get
    raise self.error
ValueError: Buffer overflow in broker connection (buffer size is 65536)
```
So now I'm trying different buffer sizes like so:
```
client = pykafka.KafkaClient("kafka:9092", ignore_rdkafka=True, socket_receive_buffer_bytes=1024*1024) 
```
... and find that I keep getting these overflow exceptions, even at 1MB, 4MB - it works at 64MB. The thing is, I'm not very clear what to choose here up front. So I'm wondering.... if we remove that check, and allow `recvall_into()` to write whatever (I think the bytearray would auto-resize?), would that be problematic? It would have me guess one less config variable (ie `socket_receive_buffer_bytes`).

(Maybe instead have `socket_receive_limit_bytes` with some big number, above which auto-resizing would not be allowed?)

issue_title: Expand vocabulary in `evaluate`
issue_body:
We should have a good way to expand a vocabulary in `evaluate` and similar commands, so that you can add words from a set of pre-trained vectors, or assign unique random vectors to new OOV words.  @OyvindTafjord has some preliminary code for doing this; see here:  https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/issues/239#issuecomment-329804862.  There are some sticky issues to figure out around how to tell the `evaluate` command which vectors to use, but that actual expand logic looks pretty good in Oyvind's diff.

Maybe we need to allow for an evaluate json file, that specifies a model archive and other parameters...?

issue_title: Discussion needed on how to best handle non ssl connections
issue_body: Currently all connections default to using https. For systems that do not have https setup, a workaround of setting the host to `http://<ip>:<port>/` is possible, but it seems like maybe we should have a config option and/or flag for setting the protocol. Perhaps call it `protocol` with the default value being `https`?

issue_title: Consider including esp_extra to EmailMultiAlternatives example in Anymail 1-2-3
issue_body:
The Anymail 1-2-3 document example is for Mailgun, but it does not directly address Mailgun's suggestion of using a subdomain to send mail.

(When you Add a domain on Mailgun, it says, _We recommend using a subdomain with Mailgun, like “mg.mydomain.com”. Using a subdomain you will still be able to send emails from your root domain e.g. “you@mydomain.com”._)

Anymail does support this setting via [esp_extra](https://anymail.readthedocs.io/en/latest/esps/mailgun/), but the example using EmailMultiAlternatives does not demonstrate the use of this.

Since the recommendation from mailgun is to use a subdomain, I recommend adding a line, `msg.esp_extra = {"sender_domain": "example.com"},` which demonstrates how to manually set this.
